data.title: Fed inspector general reviewing moves to dismantle CFPB - report
data.description:
The Federal Reserve's inspector general is reviewing the Trump administration's massive cuts to the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, according to a media report.

The inspector general's office told Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-MA), who was instrumental in creating the agency, and Sen.Andy Kim (D-NJ) that it was taking up their request to investigate changes at the CFPB made by the leadership installed by the Trump administration, CNBC reported, citing a June 6 letter from the IG's office.

Early in President Donald Trump's second term, his administration laid off almost all CFPB employees and canceled agency contracts.

“We had already initiated work to review workforce reductions at the CFPB” in response to an earlier request from lawmakers, acting Inspector General Fred Gibson said in the letter, CNBC reported. “We are expanding that work to include the CFPB’s canceled contracts.”

The Federal Reserve's IG serves as an independent watchdog overseeing the central bank and the CFPB.

In March, a federal judge issued a preliminary injunction that blocked the Trump administration from effectively shutting down the CFPB. In February, the acting CFPB director Russell Vought ordered that the agency suspend its supervision of the companies it oversees.

“We had already initiated work to review workforce reductions at the CFPB” in response to an earlier request from lawmakers, acting Inspector General Fred Gibson said in the letter, CNBC reported. “We are expanding that work to include the CFPB’s canceled contracts.”

The offices of Senators Warren and Kim and the Fed Inspector General didn't immediately respond to requests for comment by Seeking Alpha.

data.title: UroGen bladder cancer drug mitomycin wins FDA approval
data.description:
• The U.S. FDA has approved UroGen Pharma's (NASDAQ:URGN) recurrent low-grade intermediate-risk non-muscle invasive bladder cancer drug Zusduri (mitomycin).
  • Approval was based on the results of a single-arm trial in which patients received mitomycin once a week for six weeks. Efficacy was based on complete response and duration of response.
  • Among participants evaluable for response, 78% had a complete response. Duration of response ranged from 0 to 25+ months with 79% of responding patients maintaining a response for at least 12 months. 
  • In May, an FDA advisory committee voted 4-5 that the benefits of the treatment outweighed the risks.

data.title: Freight demand continues to be roiled by on-again, off-again tariffs
data.description:
The Cass Freight Index fell 4.0% year-over-year in May was down 3.4% from the level seen in April on a seasonally adjusted basis.

Cass Information System highlighted that the trade war is having various effects, with pre-tariff consumer spending still supporting freight demand. The research firm said the negative consequences of tariff effects are partly reflected in May data, as pre-tariff inventory stocking has started to turn to destocking, and those stocks are anticipated to start to thin in the coming months.

After rising 13% in 2021 and 0.6% in 2022, the Cass Freight Index declined 5.5% in 2023 and 4.1% in 2024. So far, it is trending toward another decline in 2025.

Looking ahead, import cargo at the nation’s major container ports is expected to see a surge through this summer as retailers take advantage of a 90-day reduction in tariffs that were recently imposed on China, according to the Global Port Tracker report released by the National Retail Federation and Hackett Associates.

"Retailers had paused their purchases and imports previously because of the significantly high tariffs. They are now looking to get those orders and cargo moving in order to bring as much merchandise into the country as they can before the reciprocal tariff and additional China tariff pauses end in July and August," noted the NRF. 

Trucking, freight, and logistics stocks: Knight-Swift Transportation (KNX), Marten Transport (MRTN), Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL), U-Haul Holding (UHAL), Landstar System (LSTR), Werner Enterprises (WERN), PACCAR (PCAR), Volvo Group (OTCPK:VOLAF), Daimler Truck (OTCPK:DTRUY), Cummins (CMI), Schneider National (SNDR), J.B. Hunt Transport Services (JBHT), XPO (XPO), Heartland Express (HTLD), TFI International (TFII), Universal Logistics Holdings (ULH), Covenant Logistics Group (CVLG), Saia (SAIA), UPS (UPS), FedEx (FDX) and ArcBest (ARCB).

data.title: Trump calls Musk 'a friend,' hints to higher auto tariffs
data.description:
With cooler heads prevailing, Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) shares continue to gain traction and were trading in the green for a fifth straight day as the rift between President Trump and Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) CEO Elon Musk has begun to heal.

Speaking at the White House earlier on Thursday, Trump called Musk “a friend” and has reopened dialogue with the Tesla CEO after a very public feud last week.

Although Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) shares enjoyed a brief pop on the president’s cordial remarks, the stock slipped into the red on Trump’s comments regarding higher auto tariffs and an end to the EV mandate.

According to the president, Musk did not protest the end to the EV mandate. “As long as it happens to everyone, I’ll still be able to compete,” the Tesla CEO reportedly said.

At a bill signing ceremony at the White House, Trump also said  auto tariffs may go up from 25% “in the not too distant future…as the higher they go the more likely they’ll build a plant here.” While this triggered a brief knee-jerk reaction in the legacy automakers, shares of General Motors (GM), Ford (F) and Stellantis (STLA) remain more than 1% lower.

data.title: Tech companies working on automation AI tools pose threat to ad agencies - WSJ
data.description:
Tech and media companies like Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL), Meta (NASDAQ:META), Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), and Comcast (NASDAQ:CMCSA) that are developing new tools to help advertisers automate creative work on their platforms pose a threat to the giant agency holding companies that have for decades dominated that work, according to a Wall Street Journal report from Thursday, citing industry analysts.

Michael Nathanson, an analyst at equity research firm MoffettNathanson, told WSJ that the largest ad holding companies will have to downsize in response to a technology that is speeding the commoditization of their services. “You don’t need a 1,000-person team anymore. You need three or four great people with a vision,” he said.

Jessica Serrano, chief marketing officer at Dig, a chain of farm-to-table-themed restaurants, told WSJ that full creative automation will allow brand-side marketing teams to handle campaigns on their own while looking to their agency partners primarily for strategic guidance.

Serrano said technology like Meta’s proposed products will appeal most to startups and smaller companies. “If I had an e-commerce business with one to three employees, I’d be pretty excited about a tool like this,” she said.

AI is more likely to displace agencies that focus on so-called performance marketing, since the tools are better equipped to drive consumers to a specific action, like clicking on a link or downloading an app, than to conceive campaigns that define a brand, Serrano added.

“If agencies are not quickly evolving to this, they’ll get left behind,” she said.

However, Chris Beresford-Hill, chief creative officer at BBDO, which is a part of Omnicom (NYSE:OMC), said agencies might not be easily replaced, as large advertisers that place heavy emphasis on brand perception are less likely to entrust their campaigns to algorithms.

“One thing I do know from working with the big brands, which are certainly the bread and butter for agencies like mine, is that they want consistency and brand stewardship,” Beresford-Hill told WSJ. “AI may be able to create a visual or even create a video, but the idea of developing a unique concept seems far-fetched.”

data.title: Trump says U.S. will get 'golden share' in US Steel
data.description:
US Steel (NYSE:X) +0.8% in Thursday's trading after President Trump said the U.S. government will retain a "golden share" in the company after its purchase by Japan's Nippon Steel (OTCPK:NISTF) (OTCPK:NPSCY), in the first public confirmation that the government is seeking a golden share.

"We have a golden stock, a golden share, which I control, or a president controls," Trump told reporters at a White House event, and the share amounted to "51% ownership by Americans."

However, the administration still has not specified what a golden share would mean, including - as is typically the case - whether that would include an equity stake.

The merger deal has not closed and is facing a pair of upcoming deadlines, with a court case facing a procedural deadline Friday, and the merger agreement deadline is set for June 18.

Separately, the United Steelworkers union has filed an official information request to US Steel (NYSE:X) regarding its proposed deal with Nippon Steel (OTCPK:NISTF) (OTCPK:NPSCY), the Chicago Tribune reported.

The USW leadership has seen "nothing credible" about the deal, "including whether it meaningfully differs from Nippon's initial proposal to acquire US Steel and make it a wholly owned subsidiary."

data.title: Roku set to snap six straight sessions of gains
data.description:
Roku (NASDAQ:ROKU) shares were on track to snap six straight sessions of gains, as the stock was down over 4% at $77.22 in afternoon trading on Thursday.

The San Jose, California-based streaming company gained over 9% in the preceding six sessions. The stock has risen more than 6% so far this year, compared to the over 2% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

ROKU is up 12% over the past one month. The stock closed 0.7% higher on Wednesday at $80.48.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, ROKU has a Hold rating with a score of 3.29 out of 5. The company received A+ for growth, while it got a C+ in the prospect of profitability.

Turning to the Wall Street community, 15 analysts gave ROKU a Buy and above. 15 analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, and one recommended Strong Sell.

Seeking Alpha analysts are also bullish and see the stock as a Buy.

Roku, in May, beat Wall Street’s estimates with its first quarter results, with revenue increasing more than 15% during the quarter.

A recent Seeking Alpha analysis by Stone Fox Capital pointed out that Roku continues reporting rapidly improving financials, but the biggest risk for the company is the shifting landscape in the streaming market, leading to another platform replacing Roku.

data.title: S&P rises, bonds rally after soft PPI, strong Treasury auction; Boeing weighs on Dow
data.description:
U.S. stocks on Thursday ticked higher, as traders weighed a positive producer inflation report and a strong Treasury bond auction against geopolitical risks and trade tensions. Bonds rallied on the inflation data. 

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.3% in midday trade, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was also +0.3%.

The Dow (DJI) seesawed and was last +0.1%. The blue-chip index was under pressure due to a -4.1% slide in component Boeing (BA). One of the planemaker's Dreamliner jets was involved in a crash in India that left hundreds dead.   

Before the opening bell, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported a +0.1% M/M rise in the producer price index (PPI) for May vs. +0.2% expected. The core PPI also rose +0.1% vs. +0.3% consensus. The soft data came a day after a benign consumer price index (CPI) reading.    

Meanwhile, an update from the U.S. Department of Labor showed claims for insured employment - also known as continuing claims - for the week ending May 31 hitting its highest level since November 2021.

With inflation trending in the right direction and the labor market showing signs of cooling, market participants have increased their Federal Reserve rate cut expectations.  

“If the labor market data continues to cool and tariffs aren't meaningfully passed through to consumers, the Fed would certainly be warranted in cutting sooner and faster,” said Parker Ross, global chief economist at Arch Capital Group.

“I still expect the Fed to maintain a cautious stance. They are certainly hoping the labor softening sorts itself out once uncertainty is reduced around the trade war and will likely be waiting as long as possible to monitor any potential tariff impacts on inflation,” Ross added. 

U.S. Treasury yields tumbled after the PPI report, as traders snapped up bonds. The 10-year yield (US10Y) fell 7 basis points to 4.36%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 5 basis points to 3.91%.

Fixed-income securities were also in focus after a $22B 30-year bond auction traded through, indicating strong demand. 

Headlines on Thursday were also dominated by rising geopolitical risks in the Middle East, and a renewed tariff threat by U.S. President Donald Trump. 

The U.S. government on Wednesday ordered the departure of embassy staff from Baghdad after Iran threatened to attack U.S. bases if talks over its nuclear program fall through. JPMorgan said an attack on Iran could spike oil prices to $120 a barrel, which in turn could drive U.S. consumer prices up to 5%.      

On the trade front, Trump said he intended to send letters to trading partners in the next week or two setting unilateral tariff rates, ahead of a July 9 deadline to reimpose higher duties on dozens of countries.  

Also of note, shares of Chime Financial (CHYM) popped in their market debut on the Nasdaq, in one of the most closely watched fintech initial public offerings in recent years.

data.title: Trump's crypto czar: "we're well on our way' to making U.S. global crypto hub
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump's crypto czar David Sacks said Thursday that "we're well on our way" to making the U.S. the global center for digital asset innovation, reversing a tough and unclear regulatory environment under the Biden administration.

Biden "was never willing to provide" legal clarity on crypto, Sacks said in a virtual interview at a Coinbase event. In turn, crypto innovation has been driven offshore, he added. 

Now, "things are going incredibly well for the crypto community," Sacks said, noting optimism started after Trump's election victory and his continued support for the space.

Sacks pointed to key crypto legislation moving through Congress, including the stablecoin-focused GENIUS Act, which on Wednesday easily advanced in the Senate. Aligning with the consensus, Sacks said he thinks the bipartisan bill -- which would create a framework allowing stablecoins to be issued in the U.S. under either state or federal supervision -- will see final passage and signed into law.

The South African-American investor, who co-founded PayPal Holdings (PYPL), noted that stablecoins are "already extremely successful," with the total float of these price-stable digital assets standing at more $225B.

Earlier this week, the House of Representatives pushed forward the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act, which aims to bring clearer rules to U.S. oversight of digital asset markets.

data.title: Chime Financial stock pops 59% in opening trading
data.description:
Chime Financial (NASDAQ:CHYM) shares surged 59% from their IPO price on Thursday in their opening trade.

The stock's opening trade was at $43 per share vs. its IPO price of $27.

The stock priced Wednesday evening, indicating the offering raised ~$699M gross proceeds for the fintech app company. 

By 1:00 PM ET, the stock's gain moderated to ~42% to  $38.41.
